Why, then, do you shy away so much from the sudden enlightenment that would arise if everyone were allowed to enlighten as much as they could? The human spirit generally proceeds only step by step from clarity to clarity; you will still creep along in your age; you will keep your small chosen band, and the self-conviction of your great merits. And if it does occasionally make a violent leap forward through a revolution in the sciences—do not worry about that either. Even if it becomes day for others around you; your dim eyes, and those of your protégés so dear to your hearts, will keep you in a comfortable twilight; yes, it will become even darker around you for your comfort. You must know this from experience. Has it not, since the strong illumination that has fallen upon the sciences, especially for a decade, become much more confused in your heads than before?

passages.workTitle: Zurückforderung der Denkfreiheit von den Fürsten Europens
passages.author: Johann Gottlieb Fichte
passages.text: And now permit me to turn to you again, you princes. You prophesy to us nameless misery from unlimited freedom of thought. It is purely for our own good that you take it upon yourselves and withhold it from us, like a harmful toy from children. You let newspaper writers, who are under your supervision, paint for us in fiery colors the disorders committed by divided minds, heated by opinions; you point there to a gentle people, sunk to the fury of cannibals, how it thirsts for blood, and not for tears, how it presses more eagerly to executions than to actors, how it carries around torn limbs of its fellow citizens, still dripping and steaming, amidst songs of jubilation, how its children spin bloody heads instead of tops—and we do not want to remind you of bloodier festivals that despotism and fanaticism, in their usual alliance, gave to this very people—not remind you that these are not the fruits of freedom of thought, but the consequences of previous long intellectual slavery,—not tell you that nowhere is it quieter than in the grave. —We will concede everything to you, we will immediately throw ourselves repentantly into your arms, and weeping beg you to hide us in your paternal heart from all the misfortunes that threaten us, as soon as you have answered us just one more respectful question.

passages.workTitle: Zurückforderung der Denkfreiheit von den Fürsten Europens
passages.author: Johann Gottlieb Fichte
passages.text: Oh, you who, as we hear from your own mouths, are to watch over the happiness of nations as benevolent guardian spirits; you who—you have so often assured us—make this alone the highest purpose of your tender cares—why then, under your exalted supervision, do floods still devastate our fields, and hurricanes our plantations? Why do flames of fire still break forth from the earth, and consume us and our houses? Why do sword and plagues carry away thousands among your beloved children? Command first the hurricane to be silent: then command also the storm of our indignant opinions; let it rain first upon our fields when they are dry, and give us the refreshing sun when we implore you for it: then also give us the blessed truth.[^6] —You are silent? You cannot do that?

passages.workTitle: Zurückforderung der Denkfreiheit von den Fürsten Europens
passages.author: Johann Gottlieb Fichte
passages.text: Well then! He who truly can do that, who builds new worlds from the ruins of devastation, and living bodies from the decay of putrefaction,—who makes flourishing vineyards thrive over collapsed volcanoes,—who lets people live, dwell, and rejoice over graves—will you be angry if we leave to Him also the care, the smallest of His cares, to annihilate, to mitigate, or, if we must suffer them, to apply to the higher culture of our spirit through our own strength, those evils that we incur through the use of His charter, confirmed with His divine seal?

passages.workTitle: Zurückforderung der Denkfreiheit von den Fürsten Europens
passages.author: Johann Gottlieb Fichte
passages.text: Princes, that you do not wish to be our tormentors is good; that you wish to be our gods is not good. Why will you not resolve to descend to us, to be the first among equals? World government does not succeed for you; you know it! I do not wish to—my heart is too moved—present to you here the fallacies you have made every day until now, nor the far-reaching plans you have changed every quarter, nor point to the heaps of corpses of your own people, which you confidently expected to bring back in triumph. —One day you will survey with us a part of the great, certain plan, and will marvel with us that through your undertakings you blindly had to promote purposes you never conceived.

passages.workTitle: Zurückforderung der Denkfreiheit von den Fürsten Europens
passages.author: Johann Gottlieb Fichte
passages.text: You are grossly misled; we do not expect happiness from your hand, for we know that you are human—we expect protection and restoration of our rights, which you surely took from us only by error.

passages.workTitle: Zurückforderung der Denkfreiheit von den Fürsten Europens
passages.author: Johann Gottlieb Fichte
passages.text: I could prove to you that freedom of thought, unhindered, unrestricted freedom of thought alone establishes and secures the welfare of states; I could demonstrate it to you convincingly with irrefutable reasons; I could show it to you from history; I could point you, even now, to small and large countries that flourish through it, that became flourishing under your eyes through it; but I do not wish to do that. I do not wish to commend truth to you in its natural, godly beauty by the treasures it brings you as a morning gift. I think better of you than all those who did so. I trust you; you gladly hear the voice of earnest, but honest truth:

passages.workTitle: Zurückforderung der Denkfreiheit von den Fürsten Europens
passages.author: Johann Gottlieb Fichte
passages.text: Prince, you have no right to suppress our freedom of thought: and what you have no right to do, you must never do, even if the worlds around you perish, and you and your people should be buried under their ruins. For the ruins of the worlds, for you, and for us under the ruins, He will provide, who gave us the rights that you respect.

passages.workTitle: Zurückforderung der Denkfreiheit von den Fürsten Europens
passages.author: Johann Gottlieb Fichte
passages.text: What, then, would be the earthly happiness that you let us hope for, even if you could truly give it to us? —Feel in your bosoms, you who can enjoy everything that the earth has in pleasures. Remember the pleasures enjoyed. Were they worth your worries before enjoyment, were they worth the disgust and weariness that followed enjoyment? And once again, for our sake, would you plunge yourselves into these worries? Oh, believe it—all the goods you can give us, your treasures, your ribbons of orders, your glittering circles, or the flourishing of trade, the circulation of money, the abundance of provisions—their enjoyment, as enjoyment, is not worth the sweat of the noble, is not worth your worries, is not worth our gratitude. Only as instruments of our activity, as a closer goal towards which we run, do they have some value in the eyes of the rational. Our only happiness for this earth—if it is indeed to be happiness—is free, unhindered self-activity, working from one's own strength for one's own purposes with labor and effort and exertion. —Indeed, you usually refer us to another world, whose prizes you mostly set for the suffering virtues of man, for passive endurance and bearing. —Yes, we look into this other world, which is not as sharply cut off from the present as you believe, whose citizenship we already bear deep within our breast, and which we will not let you take from us. There, the fruits of our doing, not our suffering, are already preserved for us, they are already ripened under a milder sun than this climate has; permit us to strengthen ourselves here for their enjoyment through strenuous labor.

passages.workTitle: Zurückforderung der Denkfreiheit von den Fürsten Europens
passages.author: Johann Gottlieb Fichte
passages.text: Therefore, you princes, you have no rights whatsoever over our freedom of thought; no decision over what is true or false; no right to determine the objects of our inquiry, or to set its limits; no right to prevent us from communicating the results thereof, be they true or false, to whom or how we wish. You also have no obligations in regard to it; your obligations pertain solely to earthly purposes, not to the transcendent purpose of enlightenment. In regard to this, you may remain entirely passive; it does not fall under your concerns. —But perhaps you would like to do even more than you are obliged to do. Well then! Let us see what you can do.

passages.workTitle: Zurückforderung der Denkfreiheit von den Fürsten Europens
passages.author: Johann Gottlieb Fichte
passages.text: It is true, you are exalted persons, you princes; you are truly representatives of the deity not because of an innate sublimity of your nature—not as benevolent guardian spirits of humanity—but because of the exalted task of protecting its rights, which God gave it—because of the multitude of heavy and indispensable duties that such a task places upon your shoulders. It is a noble thought: millions of people have said to me—behold, we are of divine lineage, and the seal of our origin is on our brow—we cannot assert the dignity that this gives us, the rights that we brought with us to this earth from our paternal home as our endowment,—we millions cannot:—we place them in your hands; let them be sacred to you because of their origin, assert them in our name—be our foster father until we return to the house of our true Father.

passages.workTitle: Zurückforderung der Denkfreiheit von den Fürsten Europens
passages.author: Johann Gottlieb Fichte
passages.text: You bestow offices and dignities in the state; you dispense treasures and honors; you support the needy, and give bread to the poor—but it is a gross lie if you are told that these are acts of charity. You cannot be charitable. The office you give is not a gift; it is a part of your burden that you place on the shoulders of your fellow citizen, if you give it to the most worthy; it is a robbery from society, and from the most worthy, if the less worthy received it. The honors you bestow, you do not bestow; virtue had already recognized them for everyone beforehand, and you are merely its exalted interpreters to society. The money you distribute was never yours; it was a trust property that society placed in your hands to alleviate all its needs, i.e., the needs of each individual, thereby. Society distributes it through your hands. The hungry person to whom you give bread would have bread if social connection had not compelled him to give it up; society gives back to him through you what was his. If you did all this with unblinking wisdom, with incorruptible conscientiousness, never failing, never erring—then you did what was your duty. You wish to do even more. Well then! Your fellow citizens are not merely citizens of the state; they are also citizens of the spirit-world, in which you hold no more exalted rank than they. As such, you have no demands to make of them, nor they of you. You may seek truth for yourselves, keep it for yourselves, enjoy it according to your full capacity for reception; they have no right to interfere. You may allow the investigation of truth outside yourselves to take its own course, without concerning yourselves in the slightest with it. You need not apply the power, influence, and authority that society placed in your hands to promote enlightenment – for society did not give it to you for that purpose. – What you do here is entirely out of good will, it is a surplus for you; in this way, you can truly earn merit for humanity, towards which you otherwise have only indispensable duties.

passages.workTitle: Zurückforderung der Denkfreiheit von den Fürsten Europens
passages.author: Johann Gottlieb Fichte
passages.text: Honor and respect truth personally, and let this be observed of you. – We know, of course, that in the world of spirits you are our equals, and that truth, through the respect of the mightiest ruler, becomes no more sacred than through the homage rendered by the lowliest among the people; that even you, through your submission, honor not truth, but yourselves; yet we are at times – and many among us are always sensual enough to believe that a truth gains new luster from the brilliance of him who pays it homage. Make this delusion useful until it vanishes – let your peoples always believe that there is something even more sublime than yourselves, and that there are even higher laws than your own. Bow publicly with them before these laws, and they will conceive a greater reverence for them and for you.

passages.workTitle: Zurückforderung der Denkfreiheit von den Fürsten Europens
passages.author: Johann Gottlieb Fichte
passages.text: Listen willingly to the voice of truth, whatever its subject may be, and let it always approach your throne without fear that it will outshine it. Do you wish to hide from it, shying away from the light? What have you to fear from it, if your heart is pure? Be obedient if it disapproves of your decisions; retract your errors if it convicts you of them. You have nothing to risk thereby. That you are mortal men, i.e., that you are not infallible, we always knew, and will not first learn it through your confession. Such submission does not dishonor you; the more powerful you are, the more it honors you. You could continue your measures; who could prevent you? You could knowingly and with full conviction continue to be unjust; who would dare to reproach you to your face for it? to call you what you truly are? But you resolve voluntarily – to honor yourselves and to act rightly – and through this submission to the law of right, which places you on par with the lowliest of your slaves, you simultaneously elevate yourselves to the rank of the highest finite spirit.

passages.workTitle: Zurückforderung der Denkfreiheit von den Fürsten Europens
passages.author: Johann Gottlieb Fichte
passages.text: The sublimity of your earthly rank and all your external advantages you owe to birth. Were you born in the shepherd's hut, the very hand that now wields the scepter would wield the shepherd's staff. Every rational person will, for the sake of this scepter, honor in you the society you represent – but truly not you. Do you know to whom our deep bows, our respectful demeanor, our submissive tone are directed? To the representative of society, not to you. Clothe a man of straw in your royal attire, place your scepter in his stuffed hand, seat him on your throne, and let us approach him. Do you think we will miss the invisible emanation that is supposed to flow only from your divine person; that our backs will be less supple, our demeanor less respectful, our words less timid? Has it never occurred to you to investigate how much of this reverence you owe to yourselves? how you would be treated if you were nothing but one of us?

passages.workTitle: Zurückforderung der Denkfreiheit von den Fürsten Europens
passages.author: Johann Gottlieb Fichte
passages.text: You will not learn it from your courtiers. They will solemnly swear to you that they honor and love only you and your person, not the prince in you, if they notice that you like to hear it. Even from the wise, you would never learn it, even if one could ever endure the air that your courtiers breathe. He would answer your question as the representative of society, not as you. To sometimes behold our personal worth, as in a mirror, in our dealings with our fellow citizens – this advantage is only for private individuals; the true worth of kings is not loudly esteemed until they are dead.

passages.workTitle: Zurückforderung der Denkfreiheit von den Fürsten Europens
passages.author: Johann Gottlieb Fichte
passages.text: If you still desire an answer to this question, which is well worth answering, then you must give it to yourselves. Approximately to the same degree that you can respect yourselves, if you view yourselves not through the deceptive glass of your self-conceit, but in the pure mirror of your conscience, to that degree your fellow citizens respect you. If you wish to know, therefore, whether, if crown and scepter were taken from you, he who now sings songs of praise to you would compose songs of mockery about you; whether those who now respectfully avoid you would crowd around you to play mischief with you; whether you would be laughed at on the first day, coldly scorned on the second, and your existence forgotten on the third, or whether even then the man who, to be great, needed not to be king, would still be revered in you – then ask yourselves. If you desire not the former, but the latter; if you wish us to revere you for your own sake, then you must become venerable. But nothing makes a human venerable except free submission to truth and right.

passages.workTitle: Zurückforderung der Denkfreiheit von den Fürsten Europens
passages.author: Johann Gottlieb Fichte
passages.text: You must not disturb free inquiry; you may promote it – and you can hardly promote it otherwise than through the interest you yourselves show in it, through the obedience with which you listen to its results. The honors you could give to truth-loving researchers – they seldom need them for others, and they never need them for themselves; their honor does not depend on your signatures and seals; it resides in the hearts of their contemporaries, who were enlightened by them, in the book of posterity, who will light their torches at their lamp, in the spirit-world, where the titles you give are not valid; the rewards – but what do I say, rewards? – the compensations for their loss of time in the service of others, are meager discharges of society's obligation towards them. Their true rewards are more sublime. They are freer activity and greater expansion of their spirit. They procure these for themselves, without your assistance. But even those compensations – let them be such that they do not shame them, and honor you; as free to the free, so that they may also refuse them. Never give them to buy them – you will then buy no servants of truth; they are never for sale.

passages.workTitle: Zurückforderung der Denkfreiheit von den Fürsten Europens
passages.author: Johann Gottlieb Fichte
passages.text: Direct the inquiries of the spirit of research to the most current, most urgent needs of humanity; but direct them with a light, wise hand, never as masters, but as free collaborators, never as commanders over the spirit, but as joyful partakers of its fruits. Coercion is contrary to truth; only in the freedom of its native land, the spirit-world, can it flourish.

passages.workTitle: Zurückforderung der Denkfreiheit von den Fürsten Europens
passages.author: Johann Gottlieb Fichte
passages.text: And especially – learn at last to know your true enemies, the only perpetrators of lèse-majesté, the only defilers of your sacred rights and your persons. They are those who advise you to leave your peoples in blindness and ignorance, to spread new errors among them, and to uphold the old ones, to hinder and forbid free inquiry of all kinds. They consider your realms to be realms of darkness, which absolutely cannot exist in the light. They believe that your claims can only be exercised under the cloak of night, and that you can rule only among the blinded and deluded. Whoever advises a prince to impede the progress of enlightenment among his people, tells him to his face: your demands are of such a nature that they outrage sound common sense; you must suppress it; your principles and your ways of acting tolerate no light; do not let your subjects become more enlightened, otherwise they will curse you; your intellectual faculties are weak; by no means let the people become wiser, otherwise they will see through you; darkness and night are your element, which you must seek to spread around you; you would have to flee from the day.

passages.workTitle: Zurückforderung der Denkfreiheit von den Fürsten Europens
passages.author: Johann Gottlieb Fichte
passages.text: Only those who advise you to spread enlightenment around you have true trust and true respect for you. They consider your claims to be so well-founded that no illumination can harm them, your intentions so good that they must only gain more in every light, your heart so noble that you yourselves would endure the sight of your missteps in this light, and would wish to behold them, so that you could improve them. They demand of you that you, like the deity, should dwell in the light, to invite all people to your reverence and love. Listen only to them, and they will give you their counsel unpraised and unpaid.

passages.author: Manu
passages.text: The great sages, approaching Manu who was seated with a concentrated mind, duly honored him according to custom and spoke these words: // Mn_1.1 //

passages.author: Manu
passages.text: "O divine Lord, it is right for you to declare to us, precisely and in order, the sacred laws for all the four *varnas* (social orders) and for those born between them. // Mn_1.2 //

passages.author: Manu
passages.text: For you alone, O Lord, know the true meaning of the purpose and reality of this entire ordinance of the Self-existent One, which is inconceivable and immeasurable." // Mn_1.3 //

passages.author: Manu
passages.text: He, of immeasurable power, having been thus duly questioned by the great-souled ones, honored all those great seers and replied: "Let it be heard." // Mn_1.4 //

passages.author: Manu
passages.text: This universe existed as darkness, unperceived, without distinctive marks, unattainable by reasoning, unknowable, as if wholly asleep on all sides. // Mn_1.5 //

passages.author: Manu
passages.text: Then the divine Self-existent One, himself unmanifest, manifesting this universe with the great elements and so forth, appeared with creative power, dispelling the darkness. // Mn_1.6 //

passages.author: Manu
passages.text: He who is beyond the grasp of the senses, who is subtle, unmanifest, and eternal, who is the soul of all beings and inconceivable—he himself shone forth. // Mn_1.7 //

passages.author: Manu
passages.text: He, desiring to create the various kinds of progeny from his own body, first, through contemplation, created the waters, and in them he cast his seed. // Mn_1.8 //

passages.author: Manu
passages.text: That seed became a golden egg, in splendor like the thousand-rayed sun; in it he himself was born as Brahma, the great father of all the worlds. // Mn_1.9 //

passages.author: Manu
passages.text: The waters are called *nārāḥ*, for the waters are indeed the sons of Nara; and since they were his first dwelling place (*ayana*), he is therefore remembered as Nārāyaṇa. // Mn_1.10 //

passages.author: Manu
passages.text: That which is the unmanifest cause, eternal, and of the nature of both being and non-being—the Male Spirit who was produced from it is celebrated in the world as Brahma. // Mn_1.11 //

passages.author: Manu
passages.text: After dwelling in that egg for a full year, that divine Lord, by his own contemplation, split the egg in two. // Mn_1.12 //

passages.author: Manu
passages.text: And with those two halves he formed the heavens and the earth, and between them the middle region, the eight directions, and the eternal abode of the waters. // Mn_1.13 //

passages.author: Manu
passages.text: From himself he also brought forth the mind, which is of the nature of both being and non-being; and from the mind, he brought forth the ego, the conceiver and ruler. // Mn_1.14 //

passages.author: Manu
passages.text: And also the great soul, and all things composed of the three qualities, and, one by one, the five sense organs which apprehend the objects of sense. // Mn_1.15 //

passages.author: Manu
passages.text: Then, joining the subtle particles of those six of immeasurable power with particles of himself, he created all beings. // Mn_1.16 //

passages.author: Manu
passages.text: Because those six subtle particles which are the components of his form enter into these beings, the wise therefore call his form the body (*śarīra*). // Mn_1.17 //

passages.author: Manu
passages.text: Into that body enter the great elements, together with their functions, and the mind with its subtle parts—the imperishable creator of all beings. // Mn_1.18 //

passages.author: Manu
passages.text: From the subtle constituent particles of these seven Males of great power, this perishable universe proceeds from the imperishable. // Mn_1.19 //

passages.author: Manu
passages.text: Among these, each succeeding element acquires the quality of the one preceding it; and whichever place it occupies in the series, it is remembered to have that many qualities. // Mn_1.20 //

passages.author: Manu
passages.text: At the beginning, he created for all beings their distinct names, their distinct actions, and their separate conditions, all from the words of the Veda. // Mn_1.21 //

passages.author: Manu
passages.text: The Lord also created the host of the gods, whose nature is action, and the subtle host of the Sadhyas, and the eternal *yajña* (sacred sacrifice). // Mn_1.22 //

passages.author: Manu
passages.text: From fire, wind, and the sun, he milked forth the triple eternal Veda—distinguished as Rik, Yajus, and Saman—for the accomplishment of the sacrifice. // Mn_1.23 //

passages.author: Manu
passages.text: He created time and the divisions of time, the constellations and also the planets, the rivers, the oceans, the mountains, and both level and uneven lands. // Mn_1.24 //

passages.author: Manu
passages.text: He created *tapas* (ascetic heat), speech, and pleasure, and also desire and anger; and desiring to create these beings, he brought forth this entire creation. // Mn_1.25 //

passages.author: Manu
passages.text: And in order to distinguish actions, he separated Dharma (righteousness) from non-Dharma, and he yoked these creatures with the pairs of opposites, such as pleasure and pain. // Mn_1.26 //

passages.author: Manu
passages.text: And with the minute, perishable particles of the five elements which are mentioned, this whole world comes into being in due order. // Mn_1.27 //

passages.author: Manu
passages.text: And whatever action the Lord first assigned to each being, that being, being created again and again, spontaneously adopted that very action. // Mn_1.28 //

passages.author: Manu
passages.text: Harmful or harmless, gentle or cruel, righteous or unrighteous, true or false—whatever he assigned to any being at creation, that quality entered it of its own accord. // Mn_1.29 //

passages.author: Manu
passages.text: Just as the seasons, in the course of the year, of their own accord assume their respective seasonal marks, so do embodied beings assume their respective actions. // Mn_1.30 //

passages.author: Manu
passages.text: And for the sake of the prosperity of the worlds, he caused the Brahmana, the Kshatriya, the Vaishya, and the Shudra to emanate from his mouth, arms, thighs, and feet. // Mn_1.31 //

passages.author: Manu
passages.text: Having divided his own body into two, the Lord became male with one half and female with the other; in her, he created Viraj. // Mn_1.32 //

passages.author: Manu
passages.text: Know me, O best of the twice-born, to be that Male, Viraj, whom he himself created after performing *tapas*, the creator of this entire universe. // Mn_1.33 //

passages.author: Manu
passages.text: Then I, desiring to create progeny, performed the most difficult *tapas* and created at the beginning ten great sages, the lords of progeny. // Mn_1.34 //

passages.author: Manu
passages.text: Marichi, Atri, Angiras, Pulastya, Pulaha, Kratu, Prachetas, Vasishtha, Bhrigu, and also Narada. // Mn_1.35 //

passages.author: Manu
passages.text: These beings of great splendor created seven other Manus, the gods and the orders of gods, and great seers of immeasurable power. // Mn_1.36 //

passages.author: Manu
passages.text: Yakshas, Rakshasas, and Pishachas; Gandharvas, Apsaras, and Asuras; Nagas, Sarpas, and Suparnas; and the separate orders of the Ancestors. // Mn_1.37 //

passages.author: Manu
passages.text: Lightnings, thunderbolts, and clouds; rainbows and the bow of Indra; meteors, portents, and comets; and celestial lights of every kind. // Mn_1.38 //

passages.author: Manu
passages.text: Kinnaras, monkeys, fish, and various kinds of birds; cattle, deer, and men; and beasts with two rows of teeth. // Mn_1.39 //

passages.author: Manu
passages.text: Worms, grubs, and moths; lice, flies, and bugs; all stinging and biting insects, and the various kinds of immovable things. // Mn_1.40 //

passages.author: Manu
passages.text: Thus by my command was this whole universe, both movable and immovable, created by these great-souled ones according to their actions and through the power of *tapas*. // Mn_1.41 //

passages.author: Manu
passages.text: And whatever action has been declared for each of these beings here, that I shall now declare to you, as well as their order in birth. // Mn_1.42 //

passages.author: Manu
passages.text: Domestic animals, wild beasts, carnivorous creatures with two rows of teeth, Rakshasas, Pishachas, and men are born from the womb. // Mn_1.43 //

passages.author: Manu
passages.text: Birds, snakes, crocodiles, fish, and tortoises, as well as similar creatures of land and water, are born from eggs. // Mn_1.44 //

passages.author: Manu
passages.text: Stinging and biting insects, lice, flies, and bugs, and whatever else of this kind is produced from heat, are born from sweat. // Mn_1.45 //

passages.author: Manu
passages.text: All immovable beings are born from shoots, whether they grow from a seed or a cutting; herbs are those which perish after their fruit has ripened and which bear many flowers and fruits. // Mn_1.46 //

passages.author: Manu
passages.text: Those trees which bear fruit without flowers are called *vanaspati*; but those which bear both flowers and fruit are called *vṛkṣa*. // Mn_1.47 //

passages.author: Manu
passages.text: And various kinds of tufted plants and shrubs, and likewise species of grass, as well as creepers and vines, all grow from seeds or from cuttings. // Mn_1.48 //

passages.author: Manu
passages.text: These, enveloped by a multiform darkness resulting from their past actions, possess an internal consciousness and are endowed with pleasure and pain. // Mn_1.49 //

passages.author: Manu
passages.text: These are the states of existence, beginning with Brahma, which have been enumerated in this dreadful and ever-transmigrating cycle of beings. // Mn_1.50 //

passages.author: Manu
passages.text: Having thus created this whole universe and me, he of inconceivable power was again absorbed into his own Self, compressing time by means of time. // Mn_1.51 //

passages.author: Manu
passages.text: When that divine one is awake, then this world is active; when he, the tranquil-souled, sleeps, then the whole universe sinks into slumber. // Mn_1.52 //

passages.author: Manu
passages.text: When he sleeps in peace, the embodied beings whose nature is action desist from their actions, and the mind becomes inert. // Mn_1.53 //

passages.author: Manu
passages.text: And when they are simultaneously absorbed into that great Self, then this soul of all beings sleeps peacefully, full of bliss. // Mn_1.54 //

passages.author: Manu
passages.text: This soul, resorting to darkness, remains for a long time united with the senses, but does not perform its own function; then it departs from its form. // Mn_1.55 //

passages.author: Manu
passages.text: When, having become a minute particle, it enters as a seed into a movable or immovable being, united with it, it then assumes a new form. // Mn_1.56 //

passages.author: Manu
passages.text: Thus he, the imperishable one, by means of waking and sleeping, incessantly vivifies and destroys this entire movable and immovable universe. // Mn_1.57 //

passages.author: Manu
passages.text: Having composed this *shastra* (sacred treatise), he himself first taught it to me with due ceremony; and I then taught it to Marichi and the other sages. // Mn_1.58 //

passages.author: Manu
passages.text: This Bhrigu will recite this *shastra* to you in its entirety; for this sage has learned the whole of it completely from me. // Mn_1.59 //

passages.author: Manu
passages.text: Then Bhrigu, thus addressed by the great sage Manu, with a pleased mind, spoke to all the seers: "Let it be heard." // Mn_1.60 //

passages.author: Manu
passages.text: Of this Svayambhuva Manu, there are six other Manus in his line, great-souled and of great power, who have each created their own progeny. // Mn_1.61 //

passages.author: Manu
passages.text: They are Svarochisha, Uttama, Tamasa, and Raivata likewise, Chakshusha of great splendor, and the son of Vivasvat. // Mn_1.62 //

passages.author: Manu
passages.text: These seven Manus, beginning with Svayambhuva, all of great splendor, in their respective ages produced and protected this whole movable and immovable world. // Mn_1.63 //

passages.author: Manu
passages.text: Eighteen twinklings of an eye are a *kāṣṭhā*, thirty such are a *kalā*, thirty *kalās* are a *muhūrta*, and that many *muhūrtas* are a day and a night. // Mn_1.64 //

passages.author: Manu
passages.text: The sun divides the day and night of both men and gods; the night is for the sleep of beings, the day for the performance of actions. // Mn_1.65 //

passages.author: Manu
passages.text: A month is a day and a night of the Ancestors, and the division is by fortnights; the dark fortnight is their day for the performance of actions, the bright fortnight is their night for sleep. // Mn_1.66 //

passages.author: Manu
passages.text: A year is a day and a night of the gods, and their division is as follows: the northern course of the sun is the day, the southern course is the night. // Mn_1.67 //

passages.author: Manu
passages.text: Now learn in summary the measure of a day and a night of Brahma, and of each of the ages (*yugas*) in succession. // Mn_1.68 //

passages.author: Manu
passages.text: They say that four thousand years is the Krita Yuga; its twilight before is of as many hundreds, and its twilight after is of the same length. // Mn_1.69 //

passages.author: Manu
passages.text: In the other three ages, with their twilights before and after, the thousands and the hundreds are diminished by one in each. // Mn_1.70 //

passages.author: Manu
passages.text: This period which has just been enumerated, the four ages taken together, is called an age of the gods, consisting of twelve thousand years. // Mn_1.71 //

passages.author: Manu
passages.text: A thousand ages of the gods should be known as one day of Brahma, and his night is of the same length. // Mn_1.72 //

passages.author: Manu
passages.text: Those who know the day and the night know that sacred day of Brahma ends after a thousand ages, and his night is of the same duration. // Mn_1.73 //

passages.author: Manu
passages.text: At the end of his day and night, he who was asleep awakens; and having awakened, he creates the mind, which is of the nature of both being and non-being. // Mn_1.74 //

passages.author: Manu
passages.text: The mind, impelled by the desire to create, performs the work of creation; from it is born the ether, and they know its quality to be sound. // Mn_1.75 //

passages.author: Manu
passages.text: But from the ether, as it undergoes transformation, arises the pure and powerful wind, the carrier of all scents; it is held to have the quality of touch. // Mn_1.76 //

passages.author: Manu
passages.text: And from the wind, as it undergoes transformation, arises the brilliant and shining light, the dispeller of darkness; it is said to have the quality of form. // Mn_1.77 //

passages.author: Manu
passages.text: And from light, as it undergoes transformation, the waters are remembered, having the quality of taste; from the waters, the earth, having the quality of smell. Such is the creation from the beginning. // Mn_1.78 //

passages.author: Manu
passages.text: The age of the gods, which was stated before as twelve thousand years, when multiplied by seventy-one, is here called a Manvantara. // Mn_1.79 //

passages.author: Manu
passages.text: The Manvantaras, the creations and the dissolutions, are numberless; the Supreme Being performs this again and again, as if in sport. // Mn_1.80 //

passages.author: Manu
passages.text: In the Krita age, Dharma is four-footed and complete, and so is truth; no gain comes to men through any unrighteousness. // Mn_1.81 //

passages.author: Manu
passages.text: In the other ages, Dharma is successively deprived of one foot due to what is gained; and through theft, falsehood, and deceit, Dharma diminishes by one foot in each. // Mn_1.82 //

passages.author: Manu
passages.text: In the Krita age, men are free from disease, accomplish all their aims, and have a life of four hundred years; but in the Treta and the other ages, their lifespan diminishes by one quarter in each. // Mn_1.83 //

passages.author: Manu
passages.text: The lifespan of mortals as declared in the Veda, the desired rewards of actions, and the power of embodied beings bear fruit in the world according to the age. // Mn_1.84 //

passages.author: Manu
passages.text: The Dharmas of men are different in the Krita age, different in the Treta and the Dvapara, and different again in the Kali age, in proportion to the decline of each age. // Mn_1.85 //

passages.author: Manu
passages.text: In the Krita age, the highest Dharma is *tapas*; in the Treta, knowledge is declared to be so; in the Dvapara, they call it the *yajña*; and in the Kali age, giving is the one Dharma. // Mn_1.86 //

passages.author: Manu
passages.text: For the protection of this whole creation, he of great splendor assigned separate duties to those born from his mouth, arms, thighs, and feet. // Mn_1.87 //

passages.author: Manu
passages.text: To the Brahmanas he assigned teaching and studying, sacrificing for oneself and sacrificing for others, giving and receiving alms. // Mn_1.88 //

passages.author: Manu
passages.text: The protection of the people, giving, offering sacrifices, and studying, and non-attachment to the objects of the senses—these, in summary, are for the Kshatriya. // Mn_1.89 //

passages.author: Manu
passages.text: The protection of cattle, giving, offering sacrifices, and studying, as well as trade, lending money, and agriculture are for the Vaishya. // Mn_1.90 //

passages.author: Nārada
passages.text: When men were devoted solely to Dharma and were speakers of truth, then there was no legal procedure, nor hatred, nor envy. (Nar. M1.1)

passages.author: Nārada
passages.text: When Dharma was lost among men, legal procedure was introduced; and the king was made the wielder of the rod of punishment and the overseer of disputes. (Nar. M1.2)

passages.author: Nārada
passages.text: The written document and witnesses are declared to be the two methods for clarifying a matter of doubt between two disputing parties. (Nar. M1.3)

passages.author: Nārada
passages.text: It is known to have two aspects: that with a wager and that without. That with a wager is where there is an additional stake agreed upon before the written plaint. (Nar. M1.4)

passages.author: Nārada
passages.text: In a dispute with a wager, whichever of the two parties is defeated must pay the stake he himself set, and a fine upon his defeat. (Nar. M1.5)

passages.author: Nārada
passages.text: The essence of legal procedures is said to be the formal claim. If it fails, the plaintiff is defeated; by proving it, he becomes the victor. (Nar. M1.6)

passages.author: Nārada
passages.text: Clans, guilds, corporations, and the appointed king are the foundations for legal procedures; each succeeding one is superior to the one before. (Nar. M1.7)

passages.author: Nārada
passages.text: It is said to have four feet, four locations, four means of success, and to be beneficial to four, to pervade four, and to have four actions. (Nar. M1.8)

passages.author: Nārada
passages.text: It has eight limbs, eighteen topics, and likewise a hundred branches. It has three origins, two types of suits, two doors, and two courses. (Nar. M1.9)

passages.author: Nārada
passages.text: Dharma, legal procedure, custom, and royal edict—this legal procedure has four feet; each succeeding one overrules the preceding one. (Nar. M1.10)

passages.author: Nārada
passages.text: Therein, Dharma is based on truth; legal procedure on witnesses; custom on what is recorded by the people; and the royal edict on the king's command. (Nar. M1.11)

passages.author: Nārada
passages.text: Because it is accomplished through the means of conciliation and so forth, it is called "having four means." Because it protects the four stages of life, it is "beneficial to four." (Nar. M1.12)

passages.author: Nārada
passages.text: Because it pervades the parties, the witnesses, the court assessors, and the king, each in its own part, it is therefore remembered as "pervading four." (Nar. M1.13)

passages.author: Nārada
passages.text: Because it performs four things—Dharma, wealth, fame, and the support of the people—it is declared to be "having four actions." (Nar. M1.14)

passages.author: Nārada
passages.text: The king with his officers, the court assessors, the sacred law, the accountant and the scribe, gold, fire, and water—it is declared to have these eight limbs. (Nar. M1.15)

passages.author: Nārada
passages.text: Non-payment of debt, deposits, partnership, and the resumption of a gift; also, refusal to render service after agreeing to it. (Nar. M1.16)

passages.author: Nārada
passages.text: Non-payment of wages, and likewise sale by one who is not the owner; non-delivery of a sold article, and rescission of a purchase. (Nar. M1.17)

passages.author: Nārada
passages.text: Breach of an agreement, and likewise disputes concerning land; the relationship between man and woman, inheritance, and then crimes of violence. (Nar. M1.18)

passages.author: Nārada
passages.text: Verbal assault is likewise stated, and physical assault as well; gambling and miscellaneous matters—thus it is remembered as having eighteen topics. (Nar. M1.19)

passages.author: Nārada
passages.text: The further division of these is remembered as one hundred and eight. Due to the variety of human actions, it is said to have a hundred branches. (Nar. M1.20)

passages.author: Nārada
passages.text: Because it arises from three sources—lust, anger, and greed—it is declared to have three origins; these three cause disputes. (Nar. M1.21)

passages.author: Nārada
passages.text: A suit is known to be of two kinds: arising from suspicion and arising from fact. Suspicion arises from association with the wicked; fact from the discovery of stolen goods and the like. (Nar. M1.22)

passages.author: Nārada
passages.text: From its connection with two parties, it is said to have two doors. The first statement is the plaint of one party; the reply to it is the counter-plaint. (Nar. M1.23)

passages.author: Nārada
passages.text: Because it follows the course of truth and subterfuge, it is said to have two courses. Truth is that which is connected to the real meaning; subterfuge is what is stated through error. (Nar. M1.24)

passages.author: Nārada
passages.text: Therein, a king who is a promoter of Dharma should forgive a subterfuge that has been admitted. He should adhere only to the truth, for prosperity is rooted in Dharma. (Nar. M1.25)

passages.author: Nārada
passages.text: For a king of purified soul who settles legal disputes according to Dharma, seven virtues arise, like the seven flames of a fire. (Nar. M1.26)

passages.author: Nārada
passages.text: Dharma, wealth, fame, popular support, favor, high esteem from his subjects, and an eternal place in heaven. (Nar. M1.27)

passages.author: Nārada
passages.text: Therefore, having ascended the throne of justice, the king, free from envy, should be impartial to all beings, observing the vow of Yama. (Nar. M1.28)

passages.author: Nārada
passages.text: Putting the Dharmaśāstra first, and abiding by the opinion of the chief judge, he should, with a concentrated mind, examine the legal disputes in due order. (Nar. M1.29)

passages.author: Nārada
passages.text: First, the source of law should be considered, then the topic of litigation, then the investigation, and finally the decision; the examination is thus fourfold. (Nar. M1.30)

passages.author: Nārada
passages.text: He should conduct the course of a legal proceeding by examining it skillfully, following a path that does not conflict with the Dharmaśāstra and the Arthaśāstra. (Nar. M1.31)

passages.author: Nārada
passages.text: Just as a hunter tracks a wounded deer by the trail of blood in the thicket, so should one trace the path of justice by its clues. (Nar. M1.32)

passages.author: Nārada
passages.text: Where there is a conflict between the Dharmaśāstra and the Arthaśāstra, one should discard the rule of the Arthaśāstra and follow the rule of the Dharmaśāstra. (Nar. M1.33)

passages.author: Nārada
passages.text: But where there is a conflict with a rule of Dharmaśāstra, a legal custom, if supported by reason, is powerful, and Dharma is overruled by it. (Nar. M1.34)

passages.author: Nārada
passages.text: For the blessed Dharma is subtle, hidden, and difficult to ascertain. Therefore, one should conduct the course of a legal proceeding by the visible path. (Nar. M1.35)

passages.author: Nārada
passages.text: Even a non-thief may acquire the character of a thief, and a thief may acquire the character of a non-thief. Māṇḍavya, though not a thief, acquired the character of one through legal procedure. (Nar. M1.36)

passages.author: Nārada
passages.text: Legal proceedings conducted concerning women, at night, outside the village, inside a house, or with enemies—even if decided, these may be reopened. (Nar. M1.37)

passages.author: Nārada
passages.text: Due to the complexity of disputes and the fallibility of memory, one may take time in cases of debt and the like, if one desires to ascertain the truth. (Nar. M1.38)

passages.author: Nārada
passages.text: But in cases concerning cattle, land, gold, women, theft, assault, matters of urgency, crimes of violence, and defamation, one should decide the dispute immediately. (Nar. M1.39)

passages.author: Nārada
passages.text: He who proceeds in a doubtful matter without first informing the king shall be forcibly punished, and his object shall not be achieved. (Nar. M1.40)

passages.author: Nārada
passages.text: A plaintiff should restrain a defendant who does not stand to state his case or who evades his words, until the summons is presented. (Nar. M1.41)

passages.author: Nārada
passages.text: Restraint may be of place, of time, from travel, or from work. Restraint is said to be of four kinds; one who is restrained should not transgress it. (Nar. M1.42)

passages.author: Nārada
passages.text: When crossing a river, in a wilderness, in a dangerous region, or during a calamity, a restrained person who transgresses that restraint commits no offense. (Nar. M1.43)

passages.author: Nārada
passages.text: He who, being restrained, transgresses the restraint during the period of restraint, shall be punished. If the restrainer acts otherwise, he shall be liable to punishment. (Nar. M1.44)

passages.author: Nārada
passages.text: One who is about to marry, one who is sick, one who is about to perform a sacrifice, one who is in distress, one who is accused by another, and one engaged in the king's service; (Nar. M1.45)

passages.author: Nārada
passages.text: Cowherds while their cattle are grazing, cultivators at the time of harvesting, artisans at their work, and soldiers during a battle; (Nar. M1.46)

passages.author: Nārada
passages.text: One who is not yet of legal age, an envoy, one about to make a gift, one under a vow, and one in a difficult position are not to be restrained, nor should the king summon them. (Nar. M1.47)

passages.author: Nārada
passages.text: One who is accused should not bring a counter-suit against another before clearing himself of that matter. Nor is it right to accuse one who is already accused by another. (Nar. M1.48)

passages.author: Nārada
passages.text: One should not alter the matter of the suit one has brought. One should not shift to another plea; by shifting, one loses the former case. (Nar. M1.49)

passages.author: Nārada
passages.text: And one should not bring a false suit. The penalty for a false accuser is that the fine prescribed in that case shall fall upon the plaintiff. (Nar. M1.50)

passages.author: Nārada
passages.text: Wasting time with pretexts, not speaking in the assembly, and contradicting one's own statement are the marks of a losing party. (Nar. M1.51)

passages.author: Nārada
passages.text: He who flees when summoned, or who, having appeared, does not dispute, shall be punished by the king; he is defeated in the suit by that very fact. (Nar. M1.52)

passages.author: Nārada
passages.text: In cases that have been decided, proof is of no avail, whether it be a document or witnesses, if it was not presented earlier. (Nar. M1.53)

passages.author: Nārada
passages.text: Just as the benefits of the rainy season are fruitless for ripened grain, so is proof fruitless for legal disputes that have been decided. (Nar. M1.54)

passages.author: Nārada
passages.text: Even an untrue statement, if made at the proper time, is examined. But that which is not stated due to negligence, even if true, is lost. (Nar. M1.55)

passages.author: Nārada
passages.text: He who thinks that a case has been decided and instructed contrary to Dharma may, upon paying a double fine, have that case reopened. (Nar. M1.56)

passages.author: Nārada
passages.text: In a case wrongly decided, the court assessors shall incur that same penalty. For no one ever stays on the right path without punishment. (Nar. M1.57)

passages.author: Nārada
passages.text: A court assessor who speaks otherwise out of passion, ignorance, or greed, should be known as an unworthy assessor; the king should punish him severely. (Nar. M1.58)

passages.author: Nārada
passages.text: But the king, especially, while protecting his own Dharma, must examine the honesty or dishonesty of men, due to the diversity of the human mind. (Nar. M1.59)

passages.author: Nārada
passages.text: There are men who, out of greed, would give false testimony. And there are other wicked-minded people who create forged documents. (Nar. M1.60)

passages.author: Nārada
passages.text: Therefore, both of these must be examined by the king with special care: a written document according to the rules for documents, and witnesses according to the rules for witnesses. (Nar. M1.61)

passages.author: Nārada
passages.text: Untrue things appear true, and true things appear untrue; various states of affairs are seen. Therefore, examination is proper. (Nar. M1.62)

passages.author: Nārada
passages.text: The sky appears like a surface, a firefly like a fire. But there is no surface in the sky, nor is there fire in a firefly. (Nar. M1.63)

passages.author: Nārada
passages.text: Therefore, even a matter seen with one's own eyes should be properly examined. By making matters known after examination, one does not deviate from Dharma. (Nar. M1.64)

passages.author: Nārada
passages.text: A king who thus always examines legal disputes with a concentrated mind spreads his brilliant fame here on earth and attains the world of the sun. (Nar. M1.65)

passages.author: Nārada
passages.text: ### Mātṛkā 2 *(Mātṛkā 2 is found only in manuscript P.)*

passages.author: Nārada
passages.text: > *[The second chapter of the mātṛkā included in Jolly's edition and translation is not to be part of the original Nāradasmṛti (see the Introduction to the text). Its translation is given in the Appendix.]*

passages.author: Nārada
passages.text: A plaintiff, having well-ascertained the strength of his case and being prompted by his own interest, should have the first plaint written down, being certain of the matter to be accomplished. (Nar. M2.1)

passages.author: Nārada
passages.text: The defendant, having heard the contents of the first plaint, should then submit his counter-plaint, which is connected to the substance of the first plaint. (Nar. M2.2)

passages.author: Nārada
passages.text: He may be granted until the next day to write it, or three days, or even a week. But in the third stage of the proceeding, a plaintiff who is in the right is certain to win immediately. (Nar. M2.3)

passages.author: Nārada
passages.text: A denial, an admission, a special plea, or a plea of former judgment—the reply may be of four kinds. (Nar. M2.4)

passages.author: Nārada
passages.text: "This is false," "I do not know of it," "I was not present there at that time," "I was not yet born at that time"—thus, a denial is of four kinds. (Nar. M2.5)

passages.author: Nārada
passages.text: A denial, a contrary statement, a repetition of words, and a connection to the substance of the first plaint—the reply may be of four kinds. (Nar. M2.6)

passages.author: Nārada
passages.text: Until the defendant submits his reply to the plaint, the plaintiff may have written down whatever matter he wishes to state. (Nar. M2.7)

passages.author: Nārada
passages.text: A document that states a different matter, is devoid of substance, lacks proof or a legal basis, or is deficient, excessive, or corrupted—these are declared to be defects of a plaint. (Nar. M2.8)

passages.author: Nārada
passages.text: What is receivable by a certain person from a certain person, he should obtain from that person. But not another person, another thing, or from another person—this is the threefold nature of "stating a different matter." (Nar. M2.9)

passages.author: Nārada
passages.text: "Even in my mind, I was regarded like an enemy by your friend here. Therefore, otherwise with great forbearance, I have made this complaint to you." (Nar. M2.10)

passages.author: Nārada
passages.text: That which is deficient in the amount of the object, and lacks mention of the fruit or the basis, is a defect in a document called "lacking proof"; one should reject it. (Nar. M2.11)

passages.author: Nārada
passages.text: One should avoid the defect of "lacking a legal basis" in the first stage of the proceeding. A suit of one against many is contrary to the custom of the city and the kingdom. (Nar. M2.12)

passages.author: Nārada
passages.text: In dots, letters, or words, by a single error of arrangement (?), a plaint becomes deficient or excessive and thus meaningless; one should avoid it with care. (Nar. M2.13)

passages.author: Nārada
passages.text: A corrupted plaint is one that is in a bad state, damaged by water, oil, and the like. Even if it is clear in the plaint, one should avoid it if its meaning is not clear. (Nar. M2.14)

passages.author: Nārada
passages.text: A plaint is not true, even if it is well-established, if it deviates from the fixed, practical Dharma. (Nar. M2.15)

passages.author: Nārada
passages.text: "It was given to him by me while he was on the Gandhamādana mountain." This is external to practical Dharma and cannot be established. (Nar. M2.16)

passages.author: Nārada
passages.text: By the insertion of different letters and by conveying a different meaning, the presentation of the case becomes confused, and the legal action becomes confused as well. (Nar. M2.17)

passages.author: Nārada
passages.text: Whatever a litigant, agitated by one of the passions, may say at the beginning of the proceeding, all that should be written down on a board or the like. (Nar. M2.18)

passages.author: Nārada
passages.text: For the sake of clear understanding, what has been well-considered by the dispensers of justice should be retained; the rest should be removed from the board of the litigant. (Nar. M2.19)

passages.author: Nārada
passages.text: What is agreed upon by both litigants and the remainder that is on the board, they should write down with witnesses, and also the admission of the litigants. (Nar. M2.20)

passages.author: Nārada
passages.text: Whatever is remembered again by the litigant after the writing is complete, that is called a "re-statement" and is written down in his own part of the record. (Nar. M2.21)

passages.author: Nārada
passages.text: Whether appointed by the plaintiff or sent by the defendant, whoever disputes on behalf of another, the victory and defeat belong to them. (Nar. M2.22)

passages.author: Nārada
passages.text: One who is not a brother, nor a father, nor a son, nor an appointed agent, who pleads for another, shall be punished, even if he speaks in a legal proceeding. (Nar. M2.23)

passages.author: Allama Iqbal
passages.chapterTitle: Ajab waa'iz ki deen-daari hai ya Rabb
passages.text: What wondrous piety, for all to see, He holds the entire world in enmity.

passages.author: Allama Iqbal
passages.chapterTitle: Ajab waa'iz ki deen-daari hai ya Rabb
passages.text: For no one yet has understood of man: From where he comes, or where his journey goes.

passages.author: Allama Iqbal
passages.chapterTitle: Ajab waa'iz ki deen-daari hai ya Rabb
passages.text: The very source that gives the night its dark, Is where the star receives its shining spark.

passages.author: Allama Iqbal
passages.chapterTitle: Ajab waa'iz ki deen-daari hai ya Rabb
passages.text: And as for our own tale of heartfelt pain, We tell it to our confidant alone.

passages.author: Allama Iqbal
passages.chapterTitle: Ajab waa'iz ki deen-daari hai ya Rabb
passages.text: So subtle are the preacher's sly designs, He trembles at the sound of the call to prayer.

passages.author: Allama Iqbal
passages.text: This garden of Being, do not as a stranger stare, It is a thing worth seeing, with endless, loving care, behold.

passages.author: Allama Iqbal
passages.text: You have come to this world like a spark, a flash within the air, Lest this fragile life should vanish, with full awareness, behold.

passages.author: Allama Iqbal
passages.text: I grant I am not worthy of Your vision, this I declare, But see my burning passion, my endless, waiting prayer, behold.

passages.author: Allama Iqbal
passages.text: If passion for true vision has laid your spirit bare, On every path, the Beloved's sacred trace is laid with care, behold.

passages.author: Allama Iqbal
passages.chapterTitle: Jinhein main dhoondta tha aasmanon mein zameenon mein
passages.text: Those I went searching for in the heavens and on the earth, I found them dwelling in the shadowed chamber of my heart.

passages.author: Allama Iqbal
passages.chapterTitle: Jinhein main dhoondta tha aasmanon mein zameenon mein
passages.text: When my own Reality was unveiled before my eyes, Its true abode was found among the dwellers of my heart.

passages.author: Allama Iqbal
passages.chapterTitle: Jinhein main dhoondta tha aasmanon mein zameenon mein
passages.text: Had it but known the grace of bowing in devotion, the Kaaba's threshold stone would merge with the foreheads of the devout.

passages.author: Allama Iqbal
passages.chapterTitle: Jinhein main dhoondta tha aasmanon mein zameenon mein
passages.text: Have you ever gazed upon yourself, O Majnun? For like Laila, you too are among the veiled ones in their litters.

passages.author: Allama Iqbal
passages.chapterTitle: Jinhein main dhoondta tha aasmanon mein zameenon mein
passages.text: The months of union fly by as if they were mere moments, but the moments of separation pass as if they were months.

passages.author: Allama Iqbal
passages.chapterTitle: Jinhein main dhoondta tha aasmanon mein zameenon mein
passages.text: How can you stop me from drowning, O Helmsman? For those who are destined to drown, will drown even within the vessel's hull.

passages.author: Allama Iqbal
passages.chapterTitle: Jinhein main dhoondta tha aasmanon mein zameenon mein
passages.text: The One who veiled His beauty from Moses, who spoke with God, That same Graceful One now shines forth among the graceful.

passages.author: Allama Iqbal
passages.chapterTitle: Jinhein main dhoondta tha aasmanon mein zameenon mein
passages.text: The wave of their breath can relight an extinguished flame; O God! What secrets must be hidden in the breasts of the people of the heart?

passages.author: Allama Iqbal
passages.chapterTitle: Jinhein main dhoondta tha aasmanon mein zameenon mein
passages.text: If you yearn for the ache of the heart, then serve the humble sages; this is a gem not found in the treasuries of kings.

passages.author: Allama Iqbal
passages.chapterTitle: Jinhein main dhoondta tha aasmanon mein zameenon mein
passages.text: Ask not about these wearers of the patched robe; if you have faith, just look— they sit with the White Hand's miracle hidden up their sleeves.

passages.author: Allama Iqbal
passages.chapterTitle: Jinhein main dhoondta tha aasmanon mein zameenon mein
passages.text: The sight for which the unseeing eye forever yearns— that very soul of the assembly is found among these solitary mystics.

passages.author: Allama Iqbal
passages.chapterTitle: Jinhein main dhoondta tha aasmanon mein zameenon mein
passages.text: Set your heart's harvest ablaze with such a spark that the sun of Judgment Day itself might be counted among your gleaners.

passages.author: Allama Iqbal
passages.chapterTitle: Jinhein main dhoondta tha aasmanon mein zameenon mein
passages.text: For the sake of love, seek out a heart that is ready to break; this is a wine that is kept only in delicate crystal goblets.

passages.author: Allama Iqbal
passages.chapterTitle: Jinhein main dhoondta tha aasmanon mein zameenon mein
passages.text: The one whose lover becomes beauty from head to toe— tell me, O Heart, is there another so lovely among all the beautiful?

passages.author: Allama Iqbal
passages.chapterTitle: Jinhein main dhoondta tha aasmanon mein zameenon mein
passages.text: Someone was stirred to trembling by Your gesture of 'We know You not as You deserve'; Your station remained exalted above all who show such grace.

passages.author: Allama Iqbal
passages.chapterTitle: Jinhein main dhoondta tha aasmanon mein zameenon mein
passages.text: Reveal Yourself and show them Your beauty just once; for so long, You have been the rumor among the keen-sighted.

passages.author: Allama Iqbal
passages.chapterTitle: Jinhein main dhoondta tha aasmanon mein zameenon mein
passages.text: Be silent, O Heart! It is not right to cry out in a crowded assembly; reverence is the very first principle among the principles of love.

passages.author: Allama Iqbal
passages.chapterTitle: Jinhein main dhoondta tha aasmanon mein zameenon mein
passages.text: I cannot judge them harshly; that is not for me to do, for I, myself, O Iqbal, am counted among my own critics.

passages.author: Allama Iqbal
passages.chapterTitle: Kahoon kya aarzoo-e be-dili mujh ko kahan tak hai
passages.text: How can I say how far this heart's desire is all in vain? My market's only splendor is the trade of loss and pain.

passages.author: Allama Iqbal
passages.chapterTitle: Kahoon kya aarzoo-e be-dili mujh ko kahan tak hai
passages.text: I am that votary of wine who blooms, a garden bright; The rose-scent lasts but till the cruel Saki's cold disdain.

passages.author: Allama Iqbal
passages.chapterTitle: Kahoon kya aarzoo-e be-dili mujh ko kahan tak hai
passages.text: My sweet song is the fowler's prize, the garden's only light; And restless lightning's fury is my fragile nest's own chain.

passages.author: Allama Iqbal
passages.chapterTitle: Kahoon kya aarzoo-e be-dili mujh ko kahan tak hai
passages.text: A handful of dust, I am a desert, scattered by grace; Ask not my scope, for my domain is earth and sky's whole plain.

passages.author: Allama Iqbal
passages.chapterTitle: Kahoon kya aarzoo-e be-dili mujh ko kahan tak hai
passages.text: I am a bell, a sleeping cry in every fiber lies; My silence lasts but till the caravan departs again.

passages.author: Allama Iqbal
passages.chapterTitle: Kahoon kya aarzoo-e be-dili mujh ko kahan tak hai
passages.text: From a calm heart, find means to make your tangled purpose clear; The whirlpool's knotted heart is solved by flowing water's reign.

passages.author: Allama Iqbal
passages.chapterTitle: Kahoon kya aarzoo-e be-dili mujh ko kahan tak hai
passages.text: In love's own garden, nightingale, to be silent is to die; For life itself is bound by the ritual of pain.

passages.author: Allama Iqbal
passages.chapterTitle: Kahoon kya aarzoo-e be-dili mujh ko kahan tak hai
passages.text: While youth remains, there's joy in sight and pleasure in desire; Our home's brief joy is but a guest's short stay, and all in vain.

passages.author: Allama Iqbal
passages.chapterTitle: Kahoon kya aarzoo-e be-dili mujh ko kahan tak hai
passages.text: The world decries my name, but oh, this foolish, fond refrain: I think my love's a secret that my confidants contain.

passages.author: Allama Iqbal
passages.chapterTitle: Kushaada dast-e-karam jab woh beniyaaz kare
passages.text: When the One Beyond Need His generous grace displays, The humble soul finds glory in its lowly ways.

passages.author: Allama Iqbal
passages.chapterTitle: Kushaada dast-e-karam jab woh beniyaaz kare
passages.text: O Preacher, you have placed your God upon The Throne; What kind of God would leave His worshippers alone?

passages.author: Allama Iqbal
passages.chapterTitle: Kushaada dast-e-karam jab woh beniyaaz kare
passages.text: He is no mystic in my eyes, O Sāqi bright, Who separates ecstatic joy from sober light.

passages.author: Allama Iqbal
passages.chapterTitle: Kushaada dast-e-karam jab woh beniyaaz kare
passages.text: Keep your ear always to the heart, a fragile string, For only when it's broken can a secret sing.

passages.author: Allama Iqbal
passages.chapterTitle: Kushaada dast-e-karam jab woh beniyaaz kare
passages.text: And ask the Preacher what great harm to him is done, If God shows mercy to the deedless, sinful one.

passages.author: Allama Iqbal
passages.chapterTitle: Kushaada dast-e-karam jab woh beniyaaz kare
passages.text: O God, from where does this fire in my poetry start? It is the very thing that melts a stony heart.

passages.author: Allama Iqbal
passages.chapterTitle: Kushaada dast-e-karam jab woh beniyaaz kare
passages.text: The nightingale's lament can tell the rose from thorn; Would that a world with such discerning eyes were born.

passages.author: Allama Iqbal
passages.chapterTitle: Kushaada dast-e-karam jab woh beniyaaz kare
passages.text: His pious arrogance has taught the Preacher's art: To lash his tongue at servants of a faithful heart.

passages.author: Allama Iqbal
passages.chapterTitle: Kushaada dast-e-karam jab woh beniyaaz kare
passages.text: O Iqbal, may a wind from India take flight, And make me dust upon the road to Hejaz' holy light.

passages.author: Allama Iqbal
passages.chapterTitle: Kya kahun apne chaman se main juda kyonkar hua
passages.text: What can I say of how I was parted from my garden, how did it come to be? And a captive in the snare of desire, how did it come to be?

passages.author: Allama Iqbal
passages.chapterTitle: Kya kahun apne chaman se main juda kyonkar hua
passages.text: It is a thing of wonder that I am the worst in all the world; How then was this mantle of nobility bestowed upon me, how could it be so?

passages.author: Allama Iqbal
passages.chapterTitle: Kya kahun apne chaman se main juda kyonkar hua
passages.text: On Sinai, there was a demand to see and be seen; What do you know, O heart, of how the verdict was passed, how did it transpire?

passages.author: Allama Iqbal
passages.chapterTitle: Kya kahun apne chaman se main juda kyonkar hua
passages.text: The wish to be without wishes is itself a desire; So how was the bird of my heart freed from the trap of longing, how did this happen?

passages.author: Allama Iqbal
passages.chapterTitle: Kya kahun apne chaman se main juda kyonkar hua
passages.text: Those who can truly see, see You even here in this world; So why is Judgment's promised day so testing of our patience, how did it come to be?

passages.author: Allama Iqbal
passages.chapterTitle: Kya kahun apne chaman se main juda kyonkar hua
passages.text: Could it be that perfect beauty itself caused this unveiling? The One who was hidden in veils—how did He become self-revealing, how did it happen so?

passages.author: Allama Iqbal
passages.chapterTitle: Kya kahun apne chaman se main juda kyonkar hua
passages.text: The prescription of death still remains, O pain of separation! The healer must be mad; how did I become incurable, how could it be so?

passages.author: Allama Iqbal
passages.chapterTitle: Kya kahun apne chaman se main juda kyonkar hua
passages.text: Have you ever witnessed, O discerning eye, how the rose, Born from the dust, came to be robed in vibrant color, how did it come to be?

passages.author: Allama Iqbal
passages.chapterTitle: Kya kahun apne chaman se main juda kyonkar hua
passages.text: The purpose of questioning my deeds was only my disgrace; Otherwise, all was known: what happened, and how it came to be.

passages.author: Allama Iqbal
passages.chapterTitle: Kya kahun apne chaman se main juda kyonkar hua
passages.text: The spectacle of my effacement was a sight to behold; How can I tell you how our encounter came to be?

passages.author: Allama Iqbal
passages.chapterTitle: Laaon woh tinke kahin se aashiyane ke liye
passages.text: Should I go gather straws to build my nesting-place, When restless bolts of lightning yearn for its erase?

passages.author: Allama Iqbal
passages.chapterTitle: Laaon woh tinke kahin se aashiyane ke liye
passages.text: Alas, what failure! Heaven marked that bough for its disgrace, The very one I marked to be my fragile nesting-place.

passages.author: Allama Iqbal
passages.chapterTitle: Laaon woh tinke kahin se aashiyane ke liye
passages.text: Your vision can unite the seventy-two faiths, Your single cup can serve all time and every space.

passages.author: Allama Iqbal
passages.chapterTitle: Laaon woh tinke kahin se aashiyane ke liye
passages.text: Let me create a wish so strong within my heart, That Heaven would retreat and from my ruin race.

passages.author: Allama Iqbal
passages.chapterTitle: Laaon woh tinke kahin se aashiyane ke liye
passages.text: Go, gather up your harvest, picking grain by grain, A bolt of fire will surely come to leave no trace.

passages.author: Allama Iqbal
passages.chapterTitle: Laaon woh tinke kahin se aashiyane ke liye
passages.text: O comrade bird, I honored the hunter's coming loss, Or else would I have flown to such a lowly place?

passages.author: Allama Iqbal
passages.chapterTitle: Laaon woh tinke kahin se aashiyane ke liye
passages.text: The soul-bird must not sing its anthem of the free; Ah, this garden is no stage for songs of such a grace.

passages.author: Allama Iqbal
passages.chapterTitle: Na aate, humein is mein takraar kya thi
passages.text: Had you not come, we’d have no argument to state; But in making a promise, what shame made you hesitate?

passages.author: Allama Iqbal
passages.chapterTitle: Na aate, humein is mein takraar kya thi
passages.text: Your messenger came and laid every secret bare; My Lord, in that, what fault of this servant was there?

passages.author: Allama Iqbal
passages.chapterTitle: Na aate, humein is mein takraar kya thi
passages.text: In the crowded hall, you singled out your lover’s face; Your eyes, in their trance, what a sharp and knowing gaze!

passages.author: Allama Iqbal
passages.chapterTitle: Na aate, humein is mein takraar kya thi
passages.text: There was hesitation in their coming, messenger, I know; But tell me this: what was the art of their ‘no’?

passages.author: Allama Iqbal
passages.chapterTitle: Na aate, humein is mein takraar kya thi
passages.text: I was drawn, as if by force, toward Moses’s holy height; O thirst for a single glimpse, what was your magnetic light?

passages.author: Allama Iqbal
passages.chapterTitle: Na aate, humein is mein takraar kya thi
passages.text: Somewhere, Iqbal, your name is still a whispered art; Was it a spell, your speech—what magic did it impart?

passages.author: Allama Iqbal
passages.chapterTitle: Tere ishq ki inteha chahta hoon
passages.text: The final reach of Your Love is what I seek; Behold my simplicity, what I beseech is what I seek.

passages.author: Allama Iqbal
passages.chapterTitle: Tere ishq ki inteha chahta hoon
passages.text: Be it torment, or the promise of unveiling, A trial that will test my faith is what I seek.

passages.author: Allama Iqbal
passages.chapterTitle: Tere ishq ki inteha chahta hoon
passages.text: Let the pious keep their blessed Paradise; To stand before Your very face is what I seek.

passages.author: Allama Iqbal
passages.chapterTitle: Tere ishq ki inteha chahta hoon
passages.text: I am just a heart, so small, yet so audacious, That "Lan Tarani"—"You shall not see Me"—to hear again is what I seek.

passages.author: Allama Iqbal
passages.chapterTitle: Tere ishq ki inteha chahta hoon
passages.text: O people of this gathering, my time is but a breath, A lamp at dawn, to be put out by grace, is what I seek.

passages.author: Allama Iqbal
passages.chapterTitle: Tere ishq ki inteha chahta hoon
passages.text: In this full assembly, a secret I've made plain; I am so shameless; the penalty of pain is what I seek.

passages.author: Allama Iqbal
passages.text: The plea, 'Show me Thyself!' is the headline of the heart's tale; For it, the gallows and the rope are but a childish game.

passages.author: Allama Iqbal
passages.text: If the realm of eternity marks the measure of the heart, O Lord, what must be the wine of this brimming chalice?

passages.author: Allama Iqbal
passages.text: Was it a cloud of mercy, Lord, or the lightning of Love? When the field of existence burned, it grew the seed of the heart.

passages.author: Allama Iqbal
passages.text: You would have found Beauty's priceless treasure, O Farhad, had you ever dug the wasteland of the heart.

passages.author: Allama Iqbal
passages.text: At times it seems the Divine Throne, at times the Kaaba's shrine; My God, whose destination is this, this humble abode of the heart?

passages.author: Allama Iqbal
passages.text: Qais has his own madness, and I my own obsession; He is mad for another, I am the madman of the heart.

passages.author: Allama Iqbal
passages.text: You cannot grasp this, O foolish ascetic: One rapturous lapse of the heart is the envy of a hundred prostrations.

passages.author: Allama Iqbal
passages.text: It transforms a heap of dust into an elixir, such is the power in the ashes of the moth of the heart.

passages.author: Allama Iqbal
passages.text: By being trapped in the snare of Love, it is set free; When lightning strikes this tree, it bursts into green.

passages.author: Allama Iqbal
passages.text: A spider to a fly one day did say, “You travel daily down this very way,

passages.author: Allama Iqbal
passages.text: But my poor hut has never been so blessed, That you might stop and be my honored guest.

passages.author: Allama Iqbal
passages.text: To shun a stranger is no great disgrace, But from your own, don't keep such distant space.

passages.author: Allama Iqbal
passages.text: If you would come, my honor would increase, The winding stair is there, if you should please.”

passages.author: Allama Iqbal
passages.text: The fly replied when she had heard his plea, “Good sir, go find a fool to trap, not me.

passages.author: Allama Iqbal
passages.text: A fly will never come within your door, For those who climb your stair are seen no more.”

passages.author: Allama Iqbal
passages.text: The spider cried, “Alas! You think me sly? A greater fool than you, I've yet to spy.

passages.author: Cynewulf
passages.text: Two hundred winters had wound their way, and three and forty told in number, since the Wielder of Glory, the world's true Light, was born among men to bring us solace. Then Constantine ruled, the Caesar commanded, helm of the Romans, a righteous ring-giver. The lord of legions was loved by his people, a shield to his warriors, strong in his reign.

passages.author: Cynewulf
passages.text: Then word was brought to the war-leader’s ear of a gathering horde, a grim invasion. The Hunnish people and Hrethgoths hardened, Franks and Hugas, a fearsome force, had mustered their might by the river’s margin, on Danube’s dark bank, a host of darkness. Their banners billowed, their byrnies glittered, they shook their spears, swore oaths of slaughter. The battle-king learned that a barbarous legion sought out his lands with a lust for plunder.

passages.author: Cynewulf
passages.text: Then the ring-giver roused his ranks of thanes, the lord of the Romans led forth his legions. His war-band marched, the warriors hastened, until they encamped by the current’s edge, near the foreign flood, where the foe-host waited. The king of the Romans kept watch by the river.

passages.author: Cynewulf
passages.text: Fear then fell on the famed emperor, his heart was heavy for his host of warriors, seeing the savage swarm assembled on the river’s far bank, a force uncounted. The helm of the people pitched his pavilions there on the border, and brooded in sorrow. Dread-gripped he dwelt, expecting his doom, his spirit crushed by the smallness of his company. The host was heart-shaken, horror-struck, as night’s dark shadow descended upon them.

passages.author: Cynewulf
passages.text: Then as the battle-king slumbered in sorrow, surrounded by thanes, a dream descended. A shining spirit appeared in his sleep, a heavenly herald, handsome of feature, who called out his name through the closing darkness. The king looked up, and saw in his vision, held by the herald, a holy symbol, a tree of glory, transcendently bright. He saw the sign of the Savior’s Rood, gleaming with gold, with gems adorned, a beacon of brightest wood, brilliantly shining. Letters of light were laced upon it, written in wonder, a radiant message.

passages.author: Cynewulf
passages.text: The messenger of glory spoke with majesty, his voice a balm for the leader’s bale-sorrows. “Constantine, king! Cast out your terror, and fear not this foe, this fearsome war-band. Look to the heavens, to the Lord of Hosts, and see this sign, this salvation-token.” The king gazed upward as the angel guided, and saw the bright beacon, as he had been bidden, the victory-tree, with words woven on it, shining and splendid: “By this sign, conquer. By this bright beacon you’ll break the foe-host, and quell this conflict, and crush their malice.”

passages.author: Cynewulf
passages.text: Then the herald departed, to the heavens ascending, and the helm of the Romans awoke from his slumber. The king’s heart was lighter, his load of grief lifted, his spirit renewed, his sorrow banished. The lord of legions, lightened in soul, bade his best craftsmen be brought before him, and commanded them swiftly to shape a standard, a glorious banner in God’s own likeness, fashioned as the Rood, the flawless symbol he had witnessed in wonder, the victory-beacon.

passages.author: Cynewulf
passages.text: Then the war-queen was mindful, with purpose in mind, of her lord’s true will, and with eager heart sought the land of the Jews, with a legion of shield-men, a host of warriors, to test them with questions. And so it befell in a brief span of time that the mighty war-band, the battle-brave heroes, came to Jerusalem, into the city, the greatest of companies, with the glorious queen.

passages.author: Cynewulf
